Ayahs

هَلْ أَتَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْإِنسَـٰنِ حِينٌ مِّنَ ٱلدَّهْرِ لَمْ يَكُن شَيْـًٔا مَّذْكُورًا (١)
Has there been a period of time for man when he was not anything worthy of mention? (1)
إِنَّا خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ أَمْشَاجٍ نَّبْتَلِيهِ فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ سَمِيعًۢا بَصِيرًا (٢)
Indeed We created man from the drop of a mixed fluid so that We may put him to test, so We endowed with hearing and sight. (2)
إِنَّا هَدَيْنَـٰهُ ٱلسَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شَاكِرًا وَإِمَّا كَفُورًا (٣)
Indeed We have guided him to the way, be he grateful or ungrateful. (3)
إِنَّآ أَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ سَلَـٰسِلَا۟ وَأَغْلَـٰلًا وَسَعِيرًا (٤)
Indeed for the faithless We have prepared chains, iron collars, and a blaze. (4)

Content of Surah "al-Insan"

Despite its brevity, this Surah possesses deep, diverse, and comprehensive content, and from one perspective, it can be divided into five sections:

In the first section, it speaks of the creation of man and his inception from a "mixed" drop (nutfatin amshaj), followed by his guidance and freedom of will.

In the second section, it discusses the reward of the Abrar (the righteous), which has a specific occasion of revelation (shan al-nuzul) regarding the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), which will be mentioned.

In the third section, it recounts the reasons for deserving these rewards in short and impactful sentences.

In the fourth section, it points to the importance of the Quran, the method of implementing its rulings, and the arduous path of self-building.

And in the fifth section, it discusses the sovereignty of the Divine Will (while maintaining man's free will).

There are several names for this Surah, the most famous of which are Surah "al-Insan," Surah "al-Dahr," and Surah "Hal Ata," each taken from one of the words at the beginning of the Surah, although in the narrations we will later read regarding the virtue of the Surah, only "Hal Ata" is mentioned.

Was this Surah revealed in Medina?

There is discussion among commentators as to whether Surah "Hal Ata" is Medinan or Meccan; however, the consensus of Shia scholars and commentators is that all or at least part of the initial verses of the Surah, which describe the status of the Abrar and their righteous deeds, were revealed in Medina. The occasion of its revelation—namely the story of the vow (nadhr) of Ali (peace be upon him), Fatimah al-Zahra, Imam Hasan, and Imam Husayn (peace be upon them), and Fiddah—will be detailed later.

Furthermore, it is also well-known among Sunni scholars that it was revealed in Medina, as al-Qurtubi, the famous Sunni commentator, states in his Tafsir: And the majority said it is Medinan [1]. Among those who consider the entire Surah or the part of the verses mentioned above to be "Medinan," the following scholars can be named:

  1. Hakim Abu al-Qasim al-Haskani has narrated in detail from Ibn Abbas the number of verses revealed in Mecca and Medina respectively, and counted this Surah among the Medinan Surahs revealed after Surah "al-Rahman" and before Surah "al-Talaq" 2. The author of the book al-Idah, Master Ahmad Zahid, has also cited the same from Ibn Abbas 3.

  2. In Tarikh al-Quran, Abu Abd Allah al-Zanjani narrates from the book Nazm al-Durar wa Tanasuq al-Ayat wa al-Suwar from a group of Sunni luminaries that they listed Surah al-Insan

[1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 10, p. 6909. 2: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 405.

among the Medinan Surahs [1].

  1. Also in the same book, it is narrated from the Fihrist of Ibn al-Nadim from Ibn Abbas that he counts Surah "Hal Ata" as the eleventh Medinan Surah 2.

  2. In al-Suyuti's al-Itqan, it is narrated from al-Bayhaqi in Dala'il al-Nubuwwah from Ikrimah that Surah "Hal Ata" was revealed in Medina 3.

  3. In al-Durr al-Manthur, the same meaning is narrated from Ibn Abbas through various chains [4].

  4. Al-Zamakhshari in Tafsir al-Kashshaf narrates the famous occasion of revelation for the opening verses of this Surah regarding the vow of Ali (peace be upon him), his wife, and his children [5].

  5. Aside from the above cases, a large number of other Sunni luminaries have narrated the revelation of the opening verses of this Surah (Indeed, the righteous...) regarding Ali (peace be upon him), Fatimah al-Zahra, Hasan, and Husayn (peace be upon them), which serves as testimony to its Medinan origin (since we know the births of Imam Hasan and Imam Husayn (peace be upon them) took place in Medina). These include al-Wahidi in Asbab al-Nuzul, al-Baghawi in Ma'alim al-Tanzil, Sibt ibn al-Jawzi in Tadhkirah, al-Ganji al-Shafi'i in Kifayat al-Talib, and many others [6].

This matter is so well-known and famous that Muhammad ibn Idris al-Shafi'i, one of the four Imams of the Sunnis, says in his famous poem:

[1]: Tarikh al-Quran, p. 55. 2: Tarikh al-Quran. 3 & [4]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 20, p. 221. [5]: al-Kashshaf, Vol. 4, p. 670. [6]: Ihqaq al-Haqq, Vol. 3, pp. 157-170 (citing the names and page numbers of their books).

How long, how long, and until when? Shall I be rebuked for the love of this youth! Was Fatimah married to anyone other than him? And was "Hal Ata" revealed regarding anyone else?! [1]

There are numerous other documents in this regard, some of which we will refer to when explaining the occasion of revelation for the verse: Indeed, the righteous shall drink...

However, despite all this, some biased individuals insist on considering it "Meccan" and deny all narrations related to its revelation in Medina, as well as the revelation of this Surah regarding Ali (peace be upon him), Fatimah al-Zahra, and the two Hasans! Truly, it is strange; wherever a verse or narration leads to the virtues of Ali and the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), a group raises a clamor and shows extraordinary sensitivity, as if Islam were in danger! Even though they claim to consider Ali (peace be upon him) among the Rightly Guided Caliphs and great leaders of Islam and express affection for the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), we believe this prejudice is the result of the Umayyad spirit dominating the thoughts of this group and is a product of the propaganda of that ill-fated era. May Allah protect us all from such errors.

[1]: Ibid, p. 158.

Virtue of Reciting Surah al-Insan

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), it is stated: "Whoever recites Surah 'Hal Ata,' his reward from Allah shall be Paradise and silk garments" [1].

And in a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him), it is mentioned that "One of the rewards for someone who recites Surah 'Hal Ata' every Thursday morning is that on the Day of Resurrection, he will be with the Noble Prophet (peace be upon him and his family)" 2.

[1] & 2: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 402.

In the Name of Allah, the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful

Has there [not] come upon man a period of time when he was not a thing [even] mentioned? (1) Indeed, We created man from a sperm-drop mixture that We may try him; and We made him hearing and seeing. (2) Indeed, We guided him to the way, be he grateful or be he ungrateful. (3) Indeed, We have prepared for the disbelievers chains and shackles and a blaze. (4)

Commentary

We turned a worthless drop into a human, and placed all means of guidance at his disposal

Although most of the discussions in this Surah revolve around the Resurrection and the blessings of Paradise, it begins with the creation of man, because paying attention to this creation paves the way for paying attention to the Resurrection and the Rising, just as we explained in the commentary of Surah "al-Qiyamah" a few pages prior.

He says: "Has there not passed upon man a long period of time when he was not a thing worthy of mention?" (هَلْ أَتى‌ عَلَى الْإِنْسانِ حِينٌ مِنَ الدَّهْرِ لَمْ يَكُنْ شَيْئاً مَذْكُورا) [1]. Yes, the particles of his existence were each scattered in a corner—among the dust, within the drops of the seas, in the air present in the earth's atmosphere; the primary materials of his existence were each lying in a corner of one of these three vast environments, and he was truly lost among them, not worthy of mention at all.

Does "man" here refer to the human species, including all individuals? Or specifically to Prophet Adam?

The following verse, which says We created man from a sperm-drop, is a clear indication of the first meaning, although some believe that "man" in the first verse refers to Adam and "man" in the second verse refers to the children of Adam; however, such a separation within this short interval seems very unlikely.

In the interpretation of the phrase "لَمْ يَكُنْ شَيْئاً مَذْكُورا" (he was not a thing mentioned), other views have also been expressed, including: that man, while in the state of sperm and embryo, was not a thing worthy of mention, but later, as he traversed the stages of perfection, he became a mentionable being.

In a Hadith narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him), he said: "Man was mentioned in the 'Knowledge of Allah,' even if he was not mentioned in the 'World of Creation'" [1]. In some commentaries, it is also stated that "man" here refers to scholars and the learned, who were not worthy of mention before acquiring knowledge, but after reaching the system of knowledge, they are mentioned everywhere among all people, in their life and after their death.

Some have narrated that Umar ibn al-Khattab heard this verse from someone and said: "I wish Adam had remained unmentioned and had not been born of a mother, and his children had not been afflicted!" 2. This statement is surprising because it is, in fact, an objection to the matter of creation.

[1]: Regarding whether "hal" here means "qad" (certainly), or is a rhetorical question (istifham taqriri) or a negative question (istifham inkari), various possibilities have been given, but the apparent meaning is that it is a rhetorical question, and the concept of the sentence is: "Has there not certainly come upon man a period of time when he was not a thing mentioned?"

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 406.

In any case, after this stage comes the turn for the creation of man and his becoming a mentionable being. He says: "We created man from a mixed sperm-drop, and We test him; therefore, We made him hearing and seeing" (إِنَّا خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ أَمْشاجٍ نَبْتَلِيهِ فَجَعَلْناهُ سَمِيعاً بَصِيراً).

"Amshaj" is the plural of "mashj" (on the pattern of nasj or sabab) or the plural of "mashij" (on the pattern of marid), meaning a mixed thing.

The creation of man from a "mixed sperm-drop" may refer to the mixing of the male and female fluids and the combination of the "sperm" and "ovum," as briefly indicated in the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them). Or it may refer to the various potentials that exist within the sperm in terms of hereditary factors through genes and the like, or it may refer to the mixing of the various constituent materials of the sperm, as it is composed of dozens of different substances, or the mixing of all of these together. The latter meaning is the most comprehensive and appropriate.

The possibility has also been raised that amshaj refers to the developments of the sperm during the embryonic period [1]. The phrase "نبتليه" (We test him) refers to man reaching the status of "duty, commitment, responsibility, and trial," and this is one of the greatest gifts Allah has bestowed upon man, honoring him and making him worthy of "duty and responsibility."

And since "trial and duty" are not possible without "awareness," at the end of the verse, He refers to the tools of cognition—the eyes and ears—which He has placed at the disposal of humans.

[1]: It should be noted that although "nutfah" (sperm-drop) is singular, its adjective "amshaj" appears in the plural; this is because the sperm is composed of various parts and is treated as a plural. Some, like al-Zamakhshari in al-Kashshaf, have said that amshaj is singular even though it follows a plural pattern.

Some have also said that the trial and testing here refer to the developments and transformations that occur to the embryo from the stage of the sperm-drop until reaching a complete human being. However, considering the expression "نبتليه" and also the term "انسان" (man), the first interpretation is more appropriate.

Additionally, from this expression, it is inferred that the root of all human perceptions is from his sensory perceptions; in other words, sensory perceptions are the "mother" of all "intellectual concepts," and this is the theory of many Islamic philosophers, and among Greek philosophers, Aristotle was also a proponent of this view.

And since the duty and trial of man, in addition to the matter of awareness and tools of cognition, require two other factors—namely "guidance" and "choice"—the next verse points to them, saying: "Indeed, We guided him to the way, be he grateful [and accepting] or be he ungrateful [and rejecting]" (إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شاكِراً وَ إِمَّا كَفُوراً) [1]. "Guidance" here has a broad and expansive meaning that includes "existential" (takwini) guidance, "innate" (fitri) guidance, and "legislative" (tashri'i) guidance, although the flow of the verse leans more toward legislative guidance.

To explain: since Allah created man for the purpose of "trial and testing" and perfection, He created the prerequisites for reaching this goal within his being and granted him the necessary powers; this is "existential" guidance.

Then, in the depths of his nature (fitrah), He placed the love for traversing this path and showed him the route through innate inspirations, thus providing "innate" guidance. On the other hand, He sent heavenly leaders and great prophets with clear teachings and laws to "show the way," providing "legislative" guidance. Of course, all three branches of guidance are general and include all human beings.

Altogether, this verse points to three important and fate-determining issues in human life: The issue of "duty," the issue of "guidance," and the issue of "freedom of will and choice," which are prerequisites and complements of one another.

Furthermore, the phrase "إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شاكِراً وَ إِمَّا كَفُوراً" strikes a blow to the school of fatalism (jabr).

The use of the terms "شاكرا" (grateful) and "كفورا" (ungrateful) is the most appropriate expression possible here, because in the face of the great blessing of Divine guidance, those who accept and submit and take the path of guidance have fulfilled the gratitude for this blessing, and those who oppose have committed ingratitude.

And since it is not within anyone's power to fully fulfill His gratitude, the term for "gratitude" is used as a simple active participle (shakir), whereas for "ingratitude," the term "كفور" (kafur - an intensive form) is used. This is because those who ignore this great blessing have committed the highest degree of ingratitude, as Allah has placed all kinds of means of guidance at their disposal, and it is the ultimate ingratitude to ignore them all and take the path of error.

[1]: "Shakiran" and "kafuran" are, according to many commentators, circumstantial qualifiers (hal) for the object pronoun in "hadaynahu." There is also the possibility that they are the predicate of a hidden "yakun" (be), as in: (whether he be grateful or he be ungrateful).

Additionally, it should be noted that "kafur" is a word used both for "ingratitude for blessings" and for "disbelief in creed" (as al-Raghib has noted in al-Mufradat).

In the final verse under discussion, He makes a short and meaningful reference to the fate of those who follow the path of disbelief and ingratitude, saying: "Indeed, We have prepared for the disbelievers chains and shackles and a burning blaze" (إِنَّا أَعْتَدْنا لِلْكافِرِينَ سَلاسِلَ وَ أَغْلالًا وَ سَعِيراً).

The expression "أعتدنا" (We have prepared) is an emphasis on the certainty of the punishment for this group. It is true that preparing in advance is the work of those whose power is limited and who fear they might not have the ability when needed, and this meaning does not apply to Allah, for whatever He wills exists immediately with the command "Be!" Nevertheless, to express the absolute certainty of the punishment of the disbelievers, He announces that the means of their punishment are already prepared! "Salasil" is the plural of "silsilah" meaning "chain," and "aghlal" is the plural of "ghull" meaning a ring placed around the neck or hands, which is then fastened with a chain [1]. In any case, the mention of shackles and chains, followed by the burning flames of fire, illustrates the immense punishment of this group, which is also mentioned in other verses of the Quran, combining "torment" and "captivity."

Their freedom in desires here becomes the cause of their captivity there; the fires they have ignited in this world will manifest there and engulf them.

Key Point: The Bustling World of the Embryo

We know that the human sperm-drop is formed from the combination of the male and female fluids, the first called "sperm" and the second called "ovum" or "egg."

The very existence of the "sperm-drop," then its combination, and later the various stages of the embryo, are among the great wonders and marvels of the world of creation, the secrets of which have been unveiled with the advancement of the science of "embryology," though many more secrets remain hidden.

Among the aforementioned wonders, which constitute only a small part, are the following:

  1. The "sperm" that exits with the male fluid is a very small, microscopic, moving living being that has a head, a neck, and a moving tail. Amazingly, in each ejaculation of a man, there may be from two to five hundred million! sperms, which corresponds to the population of several countries. Yet, from this vast number, only one or a few enter the ovum and fertilize it. The existence of this number of male sperms is because the sperms suffer many casualties to reach the ovum and combine with it, and if it were not for this massive number, the matter of fertilization might have become difficult.

[1]: For more explanation on the meaning of "aghlal," please see the commentary on verse 8 of Surah Ya-Sin (Vol. 18, p. 321).

  1. The "uterus" before pregnancy is only the size of a walnut! But after the conception of the sperm and the nurturing of the embryo, it becomes so large that it occupies a lot of space. Amazingly, its wall is so elastic that it completely resists this massive volume.

  2. The blood in the wall of the uterus is not in vessels and veins! Rather, it flows like channels between the muscles, because if a vein existed, it would certainly not be able to withstand the extraordinary stretching of the uterine wall!

  3. Some scientists believe that the female egg has "positive" electricity and the sperm has "negative" electricity, and therefore they are drawn toward each other. However, once the sperm enters the egg, it makes its electrical charge negative, and for this reason, the countless other sperms around it are repelled. Some have also said that with the entry of the sperm, a special chemical substance is secreted that repels other sperms.

  4. The embryo is immersed in a large sac in a thick fluid called "amnion," which has anti-shock properties against various sharp movements of the mother or something hitting the abdomen. Additionally, it keeps the embryo uniformly warm, and external temperature changes do not affect it quickly. Most interestingly, it places the embryo in a state of weightlessness, preventing the various organs of the embryo from pressing against each other, which could cause damage!

  5. The nutrition of the embryo takes place through the "placenta" and the "umbilical cord," meaning the mother's blood with all nutrients and oxygen enters the placenta, and with a new purification, enters the embryo's heart through the umbilical cord, and from there is distributed to all parts of the body.

Interestingly, the left and right ventricles of the embryo's heart are connected to each other because the issue of purification through the lungs is not present there, as the embryo does not breathe. But as soon as birth occurs, the chambers separate, and the respiratory system begins to work! [1]

[1]: In this discussion, the first volume of the book The First University and the Last Prophet and other books have been utilized.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلْأَبْرَارَ يَشْرَبُونَ مِن كَأْسٍ كَانَ مِزَاجُهَا كَافُورًا (٥)
Indeed the pious will drink from a cup seasoned with Kafur, (5)
عَيْنًا يَشْرَبُ بِهَا عِبَادُ ٱللَّهِ يُفَجِّرُونَهَا تَفْجِيرًا (٦)
a spring where Allah’s servants will drink, making it gush forth as they please. (6)
يُوفُونَ بِٱلنَّذْرِ وَيَخَافُونَ يَوْمًا كَانَ شَرُّهُۥ مُسْتَطِيرًا (٧)
They fulfill their vows and fear a day whose ill will be widespread. (7)
وَيُطْعِمُونَ ٱلطَّعَامَ عَلَىٰ حُبِّهِۦ مِسْكِينًا وَيَتِيمًا وَأَسِيرًا (٨)
For the love of Him, they feed the needy, the orphan and the prisoner, (8)
إِنَّمَا نُطْعِمُكُمْ لِوَجْهِ ٱللَّهِ لَا نُرِيدُ مِنكُمْ جَزَآءً وَلَا شُكُورًا (٩)
[saying,] ‘We feed you only for the sake of Allah. We desire no reward from you, nor thanks. (9)
إِنَّا نَخَافُ مِن رَّبِّنَا يَوْمًا عَبُوسًا قَمْطَرِيرًا (١٠)
Indeed we fear a frowning and fateful day from our Lord.’ (10)
فَوَقَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ شَرَّ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْيَوْمِ وَلَقَّىٰهُمْ نَضْرَةً وَسُرُورًا (١١)
So Allah saved them from that day’s ills and graced them with freshness [on this faces] and joy [in their hearts]. (11)

Occasion of Revelation: (A Great Testament to the Virtue of the Prophet's ص Household)

Ibn Abbas narrates that Hasan and Husayn عليه السلام fell ill. The Prophet ص, along with a group of companions, came to visit them and said to Ali عليه السلام: "O Abu al-Hasan! It would be good if you made a vow (nadhr) for the recovery of your children." Ali عليه السلام, Fatimah عليه السلام, and their servant Fiddah vowed that if the children recovered, they would fast for three days (according to some narrations, Hasan and Husayn عليه السلام also said, "We too vow to fast").

Before long, both recovered. Since they had no food supplies at home, Ali عليه السلام borrowed three man (a unit of weight) of barley. Fatimah سلام الله عليها ground one-third of it into flour and baked bread. At the time of breaking the fast (iftar), a beggar came to the door and said: "Peace be upon you, O Household of Muhammad ص! I am a needy person from among the Muslims; give me food, and may Allah grant you from the foods of Paradise." They all gave him their share, prioritizing the needy over themselves, and that night they drank nothing but water.

They fasted the second day as well. At the time of iftar, when they had prepared the food (the same barley bread), an orphan came to the door. On that day too, they showed selflessness and gave their food to him (once again breaking their fast with only water and fasting the following day).

On the third day, a captive came to the door at sunset. Again, they gave him their share of food. When morning came, Ali عليه السلام took Hasan عليه السلام and Husayn عليه السلام by the hand and went to the Prophet ص. When the Prophet ص saw them, he noticed they were trembling from extreme hunger! He said: "It deeply grieves me to see you in this state." Then he rose and went with them. When he entered the house of Fatimah سلام الله عليها, he saw her standing in the prayer niche (mihrab), her stomach touching her back and her eyes sunken from intense hunger. The Prophet ص was deeply saddened.

At that moment, Gabriel descended and said: "O Muhammad! Take this Surah; Allah congratulates you for having such a household." Then he recited Surah "Hal Ata" to him (some have said that from the verse "Indeed, the righteous..." to the verse "...your effort has been appreciated," totaling eighteen verses, were revealed at this time).

What we have mentioned above is the text of a Hadith which, with some brevity, is cited in Al-Ghadir as the "common denominator" among many narrations reported on this subject. In the same book, the names of 34 famous Sunni scholars are mentioned who have recorded this Hadith in their books (citing the book title and page number).

Thus, the above narration is among those that are well-known, or rather mutawatir (mass-transmitted), among the Sunnis. [1]

[1]: Al-Ghadir, Vol. 3, pp. 107–111; and in the book Ihqaq al-Haq, Vol. 3, pp. 157–171, the above Hadith is narrated from 36 Sunni scholars and thinkers with references cited.

As for the Shi'a scholars, they are all in consensus that these eighteen verses, or the entire Surah, were revealed regarding the aforementioned event. Without exception, they have recorded the related narration in their books of commentary or Hadith as one of the great honors and virtues of Ali عليه السلام, Fatimah al-Zahra, and their children.

In fact, as we mentioned at the beginning of the Surah, this matter is so famous and well-known that it has appeared in the works of poets, and even in the famous poetry of "Imam Shafi'i."

Here, those who seek excuses—who show extraordinary sensitivity whenever they encounter the virtues of Ali عليه السلام—have exercised the utmost scrutiny in raising objections and criticisms against this occasion of revelation, including:

1- This Surah is "Meccan," while the story of the occasion of revelation relates to after the birth of Imam Hasan عليه السلام and Imam Husayn عليه السلام, which certainly occurred in Medina! However, as we explained in detail at the beginning of this Surah, we have clear evidence showing that the entire Surah "Hal Ata," or at least "eighteen verses," were revealed in Medina.

2- The wording of the verse is general; how can it be restricted to specific individuals? But it is obvious that the generality of a verse's concept does not contradict its revelation regarding a specific instance. Many verses of the Quran have a broad and general meaning, yet the occasion of revelation—which is its most perfect and supreme manifestation—is a specific case. It is strange that someone would take the generality of a verse's concept as a reason to deny its occasion of revelation.

3- Some have narrated other occasions of revelation that are inconsistent with the one mentioned above. For instance, Al-Suyuti narrates in Al-Durr al-Manthur that a black man came to the Prophet ص and asked about "Tasbih" (glorification) and "Tahlil" (proclaiming Allah's oneness). Umar said: "Enough, you have questioned the Messenger of Allah too much." The Prophet ص said: "Be silent, Umar," and at that moment, Surah "Hal Ata" was revealed to the Prophet ص! [1] In another Hadith in the same book, it is stated that a man from Ethiopia came to the Messenger of Allah ص and wanted to ask him a question. The Prophet ص said: "Ask and learn." He said: "O Messenger of Allah, your group excels us in color, face, and prophethood. If I believe in what you believe and act as you act, will I be with you in Paradise?" He replied: "Yes, by Him in Whose hand is my soul, the whiteness of the black-skinned people in Paradise will be seen from a distance of a thousand years." Then the Prophet mentioned significant rewards for saying La ilaha illa Allah and Subhan Allah wa bihamdihi, and at that moment, Surah Hal Ata was revealed! 2 However, considering that these narrations have almost no harmony with the content of the verses of Surah "Hal Ata," it seems they were fabricated by the agents of the Umayyads or their like to undermine the previous occasion of revelation.

4- Another excuse that might be raised here is: how can a human stay hungry for three days and break their fast only with water?! But this is a strange objection because we ourselves have seen numerous individuals who, for certain medical treatments, have performed the well-known "forty-day" fast—let alone three days—meaning they drank only water for forty full days! They ate absolutely no food! And this very act led to the cure of many of their diseases.

[1]: Al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 297.

In fact, one of the famous non-Muslim physicians named "Alexei Suvorin" has written a book regarding the significant therapeutic effects of such fasting, providing a precise program for it. [1] If it does not surprise you, some of the contributors to Tafsir Nemouneh have actually performed this fast for up to 22 days.

5- Others, in order to simply bypass this virtue, have approached it from another angle. For example, Al-Alusi says: "If we say this Surah was not revealed about Ali عليه السلام and Fatimah سلام الله عليها, it does not diminish their status, for their inclusion in the title of 'the righteous' (al-abrar) is a manifest fact known to everyone." Then, describing some of their virtues, he says: "What can a person say about these two noble figures except that Ali عليه السلام is the master of the believers and the successor of the Prophet ص, and Fatimah سلام الله عليها is a part of the Messenger of Allah's ص flesh and a part of the Muhammadi ص existence, and the Hasanayn عليهما السلام are the spirit and fragrance, and the masters of the youths of Paradise. However, the meaning of this speech is not to abandon others; rather, whoever treads a path other than this is astray." 2 But we say: if we are to ignore a virtue with such fame, the rest of the virtues will gradually suffer the same fate, and a day will come when some will even deny the original virtue of Ali, the Lady of Islam, and the Hasanayn عليهما السلام! It is noteworthy that in some narrations, it is reported from Ali عليه السلام himself that he argued on several occasions against opponents using the revelation of these verses regarding himself and his children. [3]

[1]: This book has been translated and published in Persian under the title Fasting: A New Method for Treating Diseases. 2: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 29, p. 158. [3]: Al-Ihtijaj by Tabarsi and Al-Khisal by Saduq (as cited in Al-Mizan, Vol. 20, p. 224).

It is also worth noting that "captives" (asir) were usually found in "Medina," and in Mecca, since the Islamic battles had not yet begun, captives were rarely seen. This is another piece of evidence for the Medinan origin of this Surah.

The final point we deem necessary to mention here is that, according to a group of Islamic scholars, including the famous Sunni commentator Al-Alusi, many of the blessings of Paradise are enumerated in this Surah, yet there is absolutely no mention of the "Houri" (Haur al-Ayn), which are often listed among the blessings of Paradise in the Great Quran. This may be because this Surah was revealed regarding Fatimah al-Zahra, her husband, and her children; thus, out of respect for the Lady of Islam سلام الله عليها, no mention was made of the "Houri"! [1] Although our discussion regarding this occasion of revelation has become lengthy, there was no choice but this in the face of the objections raised by excuse-seekers.

Commentary

The Great Reward of the Righteous

In the previous verses, after dividing humanity into two groups—the "grateful" (shakur) and the "ungrateful" (kafur)—a brief reference was made to the severe punishment of the ungrateful. The verses under discussion now turn to the rewards of the grateful and the righteous (al-abrar), reminding us of interesting points in this regard.

First, He says: "Indeed, the righteous will drink from a cup whose mixture is of Kafoor"

[1]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 29, p. 158.

(إِنَّ الْأَبْرارَ يَشْرَبُونَ مِنْ كَأْسٍ كانَ مِزاجُها كافُوراً).

"Abrar" is the plural of "Barr" (rhyming with rabb), which originally means vastness and extensiveness. For this reason, wide deserts are called "Barr." Since the actions of righteous people have extensive results at the level of society, this term is applied to them. "Birr" (with a short 'i') means "righteousness." Some have said the difference between it and "Khayr" (good) is that "Birr" means "goodness accompanied by intentionality," while "Khayr" has a more general meaning.

"Kafoor" (Camphor) has several meanings in linguistics. One of its well-known meanings is a pleasant fragrance; it is also a fragrant plant. Another meaning is the common "camphor" which has a sharp scent and is used for medical purposes, including disinfection.

In any case, the above verse shows that this pure heavenly wine is very aromatic and fragrant, such that both the palate and the sense of smell enjoy it.

Some commentators have also said that "Kafoor" is the name of one of the springs of Paradise, but this interpretation is not consistent with the expression "whose mixture is of Kafoor," which states it is mixed with camphor.

On the other hand, considering that "Kafoor" is derived from the root "Kafara" meaning "to cover," some linguists like "Raghib" in Al-Mufradat believe that this name was chosen for "Kafoor" because the fruit of the tree from which this substance is taken is covered within husks.

Some have also considered the term "Kafoor" to be an allusion to its extreme whiteness and coolness, as common camphor is also a proverb for "coolness" and "whiteness."

Overall, however, the first interpretation seems most appropriate, especially

since in literature, camphor is sometimes categorized alongside musk and amber, which are among the best fragrances.

Then, referring to the spring from which this cup of pure wine is filled, He adds: "This is from a spring from which the servants of Allah drink, causing it to gush forth abundantly"! (عَيْناً يَشْرَبُ بِها عِبادُ اللَّهِ يُفَجِّرُونَها تَفْجِيراً 1).

Yes, this spring of pure wine is so much at the disposal of the righteous and the servants of Allah that wherever they desire, it springs forth from that very spot. Interestingly, in a Hadith narrated from Imam Baqir عليه السلام describing it, he said: "It is a spring in the house of the Prophet ص that gushes forth toward the houses of the prophets and the believers." [3] Indeed, just as in this world the springs of knowledge and mercy flow from the house of the Prophet ص toward the servants of Allah and the righteous, in the Hereafter—which is a great embodiment of this program—the spring of divine pure wine gushes from this very House of Revelation, and its branches flow down to the houses of the believers! "Yufajjirun" is from the root "Tafjir," which originally comes from "Fajr," meaning a wide splitting or breaking open, whether it be the splitting of the earth or something else. Since the light of dawn seemingly splits the veil of night, it is called "Fajr," and a sinful person is called "Fajir" because he has torn the veil of modesty and purity and exited the path of truth.

But in the verse under discussion, it means splitting the earth.

It is noteworthy that among the many heavenly blessings mentioned in this Surah, the first blessing mentioned is a "special aromatic pure wine." This is perhaps because, after being freed from the reckoning of the Gathering, the first step they take into Paradise involves drinking this wine to wash away any grief, sorrow, or impurity from their souls, and then, intoxicated by the love of the Truth, they proceed to enjoy the other heavenly favors.

In the following verses, the Quran proceeds to mention the deeds and attributes of the "righteous" and the "servants of Allah," explaining the reason for their entitlement to all these unparalleled blessings by mentioning five attributes.

He says: "They fulfill their vows" (يُوفُونَ بِالنَّذْرِ).

"And they fear a Day whose evil is widespread" (وَ يَخافُونَ يَوْماً كانَ شَرُّهُ مُسْتَطِيراً).

The phrases "they fulfill" (yufun) and "they fear" (yakhafun) and the subsequent phrases, all of which appear in the "present/future tense" (mudari'), show that this is their continuous and permanent program.

Of course, as we said regarding the occasion of revelation, the most perfect and complete manifestation of these verses are the Commander of the Faithful Ali, Fatimah al-Zahra, and their children Hasan and Husayn "peace be upon them all," who fulfilled their vow regarding the three days of fasting and broke their fast with nothing but water, while their hearts were overflowing with the fear of Allah and the fear of the Resurrection.

"Mustatir" means spread out and scattered, referring to the various and extensive punishments of that Great Day.

In any case, when they fulfill the vows they have made obligatory upon themselves, they will, by greater reason, respect the divine obligations and strive to perform them.

Their fear of the evil of that Great Day points to their faith in the issue of the Resurrection and their intense sense of responsibility toward the divine command.

They have truly believed in the Resurrection and have faith in all the punishments of the evildoers on that Day, and the effect of this faith is clearly visible in their actions.

Then, mentioning their third righteous deed, He says: "And they give food, despite their love for it, to the needy, the orphan, and the captive" (وَ يُطْعِمُونَ الطَّعامَ عَلى‌ حُبِّهِ مِسْكِيناً وَ يَتِيماً وَ أَسِيراً).

Their feeding of others is not simple; rather, it is accompanied by self-sacrifice (ithar) at a time of intense need. On the other hand, it is an extensive feeding that includes various types of needy people: the "needy," the "orphan," and the "captive." In this way, their mercy is general and their service is widespread.

The pronoun in "despite their love for it" (ala hubbihi) refers to "food," meaning that while they have a desire for the food, they spend it. Thus, it is similar to what is mentioned in Surah Al-Imran, verse 92: "Never will you attain the reality of righteousness until you spend from that which you love." Some have also said the pronoun refers to "Allah" mentioned in the previous verses, meaning they feed out of love for the Lord, but given that this point appears in the next verse, the first meaning seems more correct.

The meanings of "needy," "orphan," and "captive" are clear, but there is discussion among commentators as to which captive this refers to.

Many have said it refers to captives taken from the polytheists and disbelievers and brought to the territory of the Islamic government in Medina. Some have suggested it refers to slaves who are captives in the hands of their owners, and some have interpreted it as prisoners, but the first interpretation is the most appropriate and famous.

Here a question arises: according to the occasion of revelation, the captive came to the door of Ali عليه السلام at the time of iftar; were captives not in prison?

However, by noting one point, the answer to this question becomes clear: according to historical records, during the time of the Prophet ص, there were absolutely no prisons. The Prophet ص would distribute the captives and entrust them to the Muslims, saying: "Take care of them and be kind to them." Sometimes, when they lacked the ability to provide food for them, they would seek help from other Muslims to feed the captives, sending them to other Muslims with or even without an escort to receive help.

This was because the Muslims were in severe hardship at that time.

Of course, later, as the Islamic government expanded and the number of captives increased,

and even criminals increased with the expansion of the government's scope, prisons were established, and the sustenance of captives and criminals was provided through the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal). [1] In any case, it is clearly understood from the above verse that one of the best deeds is feeding the deprived and the needy—not only the needy Muslims, but even the captives of the lands of polytheism are covered by this Islamic instruction, to the extent that feeding them is counted as one of the prominent deeds of the "righteous."

In a Hadith from the Messenger of Allah ص, we read: "Treat the captives with kindness." When the Muslims heard this, they would sometimes give their own food to the captive and prioritize him over themselves. 2

The fourth prominent deed of the righteous is identified as sincerity (ikhlas), as He says: "They say: We feed you only for the sake of Allah; we desire from you neither reward nor thanks" (إِنَّما نُطْعِمُكُمْ لِوَجْهِ اللَّهِ لا نُرِيدُ مِنْكُمْ جَزاءً وَ لا شُكُوراً).

This program is not limited to the matter of feeding; rather, all their deeds are sincere and for the sake of the Pure Essence of Allah, with no expectation of reward from people or even their appreciation and thanks. Fundamentally, in Islam, the value of an action lies in the sincerity of intention; otherwise, actions driven by non-divine motives—whether hypocritical, for the sake of self-desire, for the thanks and appreciation of people, or for material reward—have no spiritual or divine value. The famous Hadith of the Prophet ص:

"There is no action except by intention, and indeed actions are by intentions"

refers to this very meaning.

The meaning of "Wajh Allah" (the Face of Allah) is the Essence of Allah; otherwise, Allah does not have a physical "face." This is the same point emphasized in other verses of the Quran. In Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 272, we read: "And whatever you spend, spend it not except seeking the Face of Allah." And in Surah Al-Kahf, verse 28, describing the worthy companions of the Prophet ص, it says: "And keep yourself patient with those who call upon their Lord morning and evening, seeking His Face."

In the final description of the "righteous," He says: "They say: Indeed, we fear from our Lord a Day that is frowning and harsh" (إِنَّا نَخافُ مِنْ رَبِّنا يَوْماً عَبُوساً قَمْطَرِيراً).

This statement may be the "state of their being" (lisan al-hal) or their "actual speech" (lisan al-qal).

Describing the Day of Resurrection as a "frowning" (abus) and "harsh" day—even though "frowning" is a human attribute used for someone who scowls—is to emphasize the terrifying nature of that Day. That is, the events of that Day are so harsh and distressing that not only are humans frowning on that Day, but it is as if the Day itself is frowning.

Regarding the root from which "Qamtareer" is derived, there is discussion among commentators and linguists. Some consider it from "Qamtar," and some consider it derived from the root "Qatar" (rhyming with murgh) with the meem being extra.

However, the famous view is the first one, meaning severe and frowning. [1]

[1]: Al-Mufradat by Raghib, Lisan al-Arab, Al-Munjid, Qurtubi, and Majma' al-Bayan.

A question arises here: if the "righteous" act only for the sake of the Pure Essence of Allah, then why do they say, "We fear the punishment of the Day of Resurrection"? Is a divine motive compatible with the motive of fearing the punishment of the Resurrection?! However, by noting one point, the answer to this question becomes clear: they, in any case, take steps for the sake of Allah. If they fear the punishment of the Resurrection, it is because it is the punishment of Allah; and if they desire the blessings of Paradise, it is because these blessings are from Him. This is the same point raised in the chapter on "Intention of Worship" in jurisprudence, where it is said: the intention of seeking proximity (qurbat) in worship does not contradict the motive of desiring reward, fearing punishment, or even seeking the material favors of this world from Allah (such as the prayer for rain), because all of these return to Allah. It is, so to speak, a "motive upon a motive," although the highest stage of worship is that neither the desire for the blessings of Paradise nor the fear of the punishment of Hell is its motive, but rather it is performed entirely as "love for Allah" (hubban lillah).

The expression "Indeed, we fear from our Lord a Day that is frowning and harsh" is also evidence that this fear is also fear of the Lord.

A noteworthy point is that the second attribute among the five, and the fifth attribute, are both the issue of fear, with the difference that in the first, only the fear of the Day of Resurrection is mentioned, while in the second, it is the fear of the Lord on the Day of Resurrection. In one instance, the Day of Resurrection is described as having widespread evil, and in the other, as frowning and harsh, which in reality conveys its quantitative extensiveness in one and its qualitative intensity in the other.

In the final verse under discussion, referring to the summary result of the good deeds and pure intentions that

the "righteous" possess, He says: "So Allah will protect them from the evil of that Day and will meet them with radiance and happiness" (فَوَقاهُمُ اللَّهُ شَرَّ ذلِكَ الْيَوْمِ وَ لَقَّاهُمْ‌ نظرة و سرورا).

"Nadhrah" means a special freshness, bloom, and vibrancy that comes to a person as a result of abundance of blessing and comfort. Yes, the color of their faces on that Day will announce their inner peace and joy.

Therefore, if in this world they were fearful of that Day out of a sense of responsibility, Allah, in exchange, will drown them in happiness and joy on that Day.

The expression "laqqahum" (He will meet them) is a very beautiful expression showing that the Great Allah will welcome these esteemed guests with His special grace and place them, drowned in joy and happiness, under the shadow of His mercy.

Key Point: Satiating the Hungry is Among the Best Virtues

Not only do the verses under discussion count the feeding of food as one of the prominent deeds of the righteous and the servants of Allah, but this meaning is emphasized and stressed in many verses of the Quran, showing that this act has a special belovedness in the presence of Allah.

If we look at today's world, where according to published news, millions of people die of hunger every year, while in other parts of the world so much extra food is thrown into trash bins that it is beyond calculation, the importance of this Islamic instruction on one hand, and the distance of today's world from moral standards on the other, becomes clear.

In Islamic narrations, great emphasis is also seen in this regard, of which we bring a few Hadiths here as examples:

1- In a Hadith from the Prophet ص, it is stated:

"Whoever feeds three people from among the Muslims, Allah will feed him from three gardens of Paradise in the Kingdom of the Heavens." [1] And in a Hadith from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Whoever feeds a believer until he is satiated, none of Allah's creation knows what reward he has in the Hereafter—neither a proximate angel nor a sent prophet—except Allah, the Lord of the Worlds." 2 In another Hadith from the same Imam, it is narrated that he said:

"That I feed a needy believer is more beloved to me than visiting him, and that I visit him is more beloved to me than freeing ten slaves!" [3] It is noteworthy that the narrations do not only emphasize the needy and the hungry; rather, in some, it is explicitly stated that feeding believers, even if they are not in need, is like freeing a slave. This shows that the goal of this act, in addition to meeting needs, is to attract love and strengthen the bonds of friendship and sincerity—unlike

what is common in today's material world, where sometimes two close friends or two relatives go to a restaurant and each must pay his own share, as if the matter of hosting guests, especially many people, is very surprising to them.

In some narrations, it is also specified that feeding the hungry in an absolute sense (even if they are not believers or Muslims) is among the most virtuous deeds, as in a narration from the noble Prophet of Islam ص, it is stated that he said:

"Among the most virtuous deeds with Allah is cooling hot livers and satiating hungry stomachs. By Him in Whose hand is the soul of Muhammad ص, no servant has believed in me who sleeps satiated while his brother—or he said his neighbor—the Muslim is hungry!" [1] Although the end of the above Hadith is about satiating Muslims, its beginning includes every thirsty and hungry being, and it is not unlikely that the breadth of its concept even includes animals.

And there are many narrations in this regard. 2

[1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter "Feeding the Believer," Hadith 3. 2: Ibid., Hadith 6. [3]: Ibid., Hadith 18. [1] & 2: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 74, p. 369. It is noteworthy that the late Allamah Majlisi has titled a chapter in this regard containing 113 Hadiths related to feeding the believer, giving them drink, clothing them, and paying their debts, some of which are general.
