Commentary

Great Heavenly Rewards!

After the brief reference in the preceding verses to the salvation of the "Righteous" (Abrār) from the painful punishments of the Day of Resurrection, and their attainment of the meeting with the Beloved, being immersed in joy and happiness, the verses under discussion proceed to detail these heavenly blessings, enumerating at least fifteen favors throughout these verses:

First, it speaks of the dwelling and clothing of these inhabitants of Paradise, stating:

"And He rewarded them for their patience and steadfastness with a Garden and garments and carpets of silk" (وَ جَزاهُمْ بِما صَبَرُوا جَنَّةً وَ حَرِيراً).

Indeed, in exchange for all that endurance and self-sacrifice—exemplified by fulfilling vows, fasting, and giving away their own needed food at the time of breaking fast to the needy, the orphan, and the captive—Allah settles them in special gardens of Paradise and clothes them in the finest garments.

Not only in this verse but in other verses of the Quran as well, it is explicitly stated that the rewards of the Hereafter are in exchange for human patience and steadfastness (patience in the path of obedience, patience against sin, and patience and endurance in the face of hardships and calamities).

In Surah Ar-Ra'd, verse 24, we read that the angels welcome the inhabitants of Paradise saying: سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ بِما صَبَرْتُمْ—"Peace be upon you for the patience and steadfastness you maintained."

And in Surah Al-Mu'minun, verse 111, it states: إِنِّي جَزَيْتُهُمُ الْيَوْمَ بِما صَبَرُوا أَنَّهُمْ هُمُ الْفائِزُونَ—"Indeed, I have rewarded them this Day for their patience and steadfastness; they are the successful ones."

Then it adds: "This is while they are reclining therein upon beautiful couches; they see therein neither the heat of the sun nor the intense cold" (مُتَّكِئِينَ فِيها عَلَى الْأَرائِكِ لا يَرَوْنَ فِيها شَمْساً وَ لا زَمْهَرِيراً).

The mention of this state (reclining on couches) points to their complete peace and comfort, as a person usually remains in such a state when at rest. The end of the verse also refers to the perfect moderation of the climate in Paradise.

It does not mean that the sun and moon do not exist there, but rather that the uncomfortable glare of the sun does not exist, given the presence of the shadows of the heavenly trees.

"Zamharīr" is derived from the root "zamhar," meaning intense cold, or intense anger, or the reddening of the eyes due to rage; here, the first meaning is intended.

In a Hadith, it is mentioned that there is a spot in Hell where the limbs of the body disintegrate due to the intensity of the cold. [^1] "Arā'ik" is the plural of "arīkah," which originally refers to the thrones or couches placed in a bridal chamber, and here it refers to beautiful and magnificent thrones.

"Alusi," the famous Sunni commentator, in Ruh al-Ma'ani, narrates a Hadith from Ibn Abbas as follows: "While the people of Paradise are in Paradise, they suddenly see a light like the light of the sun, by which the gardens are illuminated. The people of Paradise ask: 'O Ridwan (the guardian of Paradise), what is this?

While our Lord has said: لا يَرَوْنَ فِيها شَمْساً وَ لا زَمْهَرِيراً (they see therein neither sun nor intense cold).' Ridwan replies to them: 'This is neither the sun nor the moon, but Ali (عليه السلام) and Fatimah (عليها السلام) have smiled, and the gardens have been illuminated by the light of their teeth!'" [^1]

[^1]: Ad-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 300.

The next verse, continuing the description of these blessings, adds: "And the shadows of the heavenly trees are hanging low over them, and its clusters of fruits are brought near, very easy to reach": (وَ دانِيَةً عَلَيْهِمْ ظِلالُها وَ ذُلِّلَتْ قُطُوفُها تَذْلِيلًا) [^2]. There is no difficulty, no thorn enters the hand, and there is no need for effort or movement to pick the fruits! Once again, we find it necessary to remind that the principles governing human life in that world are very different from this world. What has been mentioned regarding heavenly blessings in these verses and other verses of the Quran are merely meaningful indications of those great favors; otherwise, according to some narrations, there are blessings there that no eye has seen, no ear has heard, and has never crossed the mind of any human being.

"Ibn Abbas" has a statement mentioned under some verses of this same Surah: He says: "Whatever Allah has named in the Quran regarding heavenly blessings has no like or equal in this world, but Allah names them with names known to us. For example, He mentions a pure drink mixed with 'ginger' (zanjabīl); this was a fragrant substance that the Arabs were fond of." [^1]

[^1]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 29, p. 159. [^2]: "Qutūf" is the plural of "qitf," meaning picked fruits or the act of picking fruit.

In the next verse, He explains part of the manner in which these heavenly guests of God are hosted, their serving vessels, and the servers, saying: "And there will be circulated among them vessels of silver and bowls of crystal" (وَ يُطافُ عَلَيْهِمْ بِآنِيَةٍ مِنْ فِضَّةٍ وَ أَكْوابٍ كانَتْ قَوارِيرَا).

"Crystal-clear vessels made of silver! Which they have prepared in due measure" (قَوارِيرَا مِنْ فِضَّةٍ قَدَّرُوها تَقْدِيراً). In these vessels are various types of heavenly foods, and in those crystal bowls are various types of delightful and refreshing drinks, in the exact amount they desire and like, and the heavenly servants constantly circulate around them and offer them.

"Āniyah" is the plural of "inā’," meaning any kind of vessel. "Akwāb" is the plural of "kūb," meaning a water vessel without a handle, sometimes referred to as a "goblet" (qadah).

"Qawārīr" is the plural of "qārūrah," meaning a vessel made of crystal or glass.

The wonder is that He says: the heavenly crystal vessels are made of silver! While in the world, such a vessel absolutely does not exist; crystal vessels are made from specific stones that are melted. However, the same God who created the potential in dark stone to be transformed into glass and crystal can also create it in a metal like silver.

In any case, it is understood from this expression that the heavenly vessels and cups have both the clarity and transparency of crystal and the brilliance and beauty of silver, and the drinks within them are fully visible.

It is noteworthy that in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), it is stated: "The sight of the human eye penetrates through the silver of Paradise just as it penetrates through the glass and crystal of the world!" [^1] We also know that in our era, scientists have discovered rays (such as X-rays) that pass through opaque objects and show the interior as if it were crystal.

Ibn Abbas says: "All heavenly blessings have a likeness and parallel in the world except for the crystal vessels made of silver, for which there is no likeness or parallel in the world." [^2]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 411. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 410. [^3]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 29, p. 159.

Then He adds: "And they will be given to drink therein a cup [of wine] whose mixture is of ginger" (وَ يُسْقَوْنَ فِيها كَأْساً كانَ مِزاجُها زَنْجَبِيلًا). Many commentators have explicitly stated that the pre-Islamic Arabs enjoyed drinks mixed with ginger because it gave the drink a specific pungency. Here, the Quran speaks of cups whose pure drink is mixed with ginger, but it is obvious that the difference between this drink and that drink is as far as the earth is from the heavens! Or in other words, the difference is between this world and the Hereafter!

It seems that the Arabs had two types of drinks with two different states: one supposedly refreshing and stimulating, and the other relaxing and soothing. They mixed the first with "ginger" and the second with "camphor" (kāfūr). Since the realities of the world of the Hereafter cannot be contained within the words of this world, there is no choice but to employ these words with broader and loftier meanings for those great realities.

Although various interpretations have been narrated regarding the meaning of "zanjabīl" (ginger), it is mostly interpreted as that fragrant and pleasant root used in specific food spices and drinks.

Then He adds: "These cups are filled from a spring in Paradise called Salsabīl" (عَيْناً فِيها تُسَمَّى سَلْسَبِيلًا) [^1]. "Salsabīl" refers to a very delicious drink that flows easily in the mouth and throat and is perfectly wholesome. Many believe it is derived from the root "salāsah," meaning "fluidity," just as fluent and attractive speech is called "salīs." Others have said it is derived from the root "tasalsul," meaning "continuous movement," which consequently evokes the fluidity of something. Therefore, both meanings are close to each other.

Some also believe that this is a compound word made of two words: "sal" (ask) and "sabīl" (a path). In the first case, the concept is "ask for a path," and in the second, "he asked for a path," and the metaphorical meaning of both is "wholesome."

Some have also explicitly stated that the word "Salsabīl" did not exist in the Arabic language and is one of the innovations of the Holy Quran. [^1] However, the first meaning is the most famous and appropriate.

[^1]: Regarding the grammatical position of "aynan," the same applies as mentioned a few verses ago; it is most appropriate to be "accusative by the removal of the preposition." [^2]: Some commentators have said that "Salsabīl" should normally be indeclinable (ghayr munsarif), but it takes nunnation (tanwīn) here for harmony with the endings of the other verses in this Surah.

Then He speaks of the servers of this joyful banquet held in the proximity of God's mercy in the highest Paradise, saying: "And there will circulate among them young boys of everlasting youth; when you see them, you would think them scattered pearls!" (وَ يَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ وِلْدانٌ مُخَلَّدُونَ إِذا رَأَيْتَهُمْ حَسِبْتَهُمْ لُؤْلُؤاً مَنْثُوراً). Both they themselves are eternal in Paradise, and their freshness, beauty, and youthful vigor are eternal, as is their service; for the expression "mukhalladūn" (everlasting) on one hand, and the expression "يَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ" (circulating among them) on the other, express this reality.

The comparison to "لُؤْلُؤاً مَنْثُوراً" (scattered pearls) is an indication of their beauty, purity, brilliance, and attractiveness, as well as their presence everywhere in this divine and spiritual banquet.

And since the blessings of the other world cannot be described, no matter how expressive and eloquent the words may be, in the next verse He adds in a general manner: "And when you look there, you will see blessings and a great kingdom!" (وَ إِذا رَأَيْتَ ثَمَّ رَأَيْتَ نَعِيماً وَ مُلْكاً كَبِيراً) [^1]. Many interpretations have been given for "na'īm" (blessings) and "mulkan kabīran" (a great kingdom), including a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) where we read: "The meaning of the verse is a kingdom that never wanes and never perishes." [^2] Or that the heavenly blessings are so vast that they cannot be described in terms of quantity. Or that the "great kingdom" is such that the angels seek permission when entering upon the inhabitants of Paradise and greet them with peace. Or that the inhabitants of Paradise attain whatever they desire. Or that the lowest person in Paradise has a realm so vast that when he looks, he sees a distance of a thousand years' journey! Or it means a permanent and eternal kingdom accompanied by the realization of all wishes.

The word "na'īm," which linguistically means "abundant blessings," and "mulkan kabīran," which informs of the greatness and vastness of the gardens of the inhabitants of Paradise, have broad and expansive concepts that include all the above interpretations.

[^1]: Some commentators have specified that "thamma" here is an "adverb of place" and "ra'ayta" has an intransitive meaning, so the verse means: "When you cast your sight there, you will see blessings..." It is also possible that "thamma" is a "demonstrative pronoun for the distant" and the object of "ra'ayta." [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 411.

Up to this point, reference has been made to a portion of the heavenly blessings such as "dwellings," "couches," "shadows," "fruits," "drinks," "vessels," and "the group of servers." Now it is the turn of the decorative items of the inhabitants of Paradise; He says: "Upon them will be garments of fine green silk and heavy brocade" (عالِيَهُمْ ثِيابُ سُندُسٍ خُضْرٌ وَ إِسْتَبْرَقٌ) [^1]. "Sundus" means thin silk fabric, while "istabraq" means thick silk fabric. Some consider it to be from the Persian word "astabar" or "satabr" (thick), and some have suggested it is from the Arabic root "barq" meaning brilliance. Then He adds: "And they will be adorned with bracelets of silver" (وَ حُلُّوا أَساوِرَ مِنْ فِضَّةٍ). Transparent silver that shines like crystal and is more beautiful than rubies, pearls, and gems.

"Asāwir" is the plural of "aswirah," which in turn is the plural of "siwār," originally taken from the Persian word "dastvār" meaning "bracelet," and upon being transferred to the Arabic language, it underwent a slight change and became "siwār."

[^1]: Regarding the grammatical position of "'āliyahum," there are two possibilities: first, that it is an "adverb" meaning "above," thus the concept is "above them are garments of silk." Second, that it is a "circumstantial qualifier" (hāl) for the pronoun "them" referring to the "Righteous."

The choice of the color green for heavenly garments is because it is a very refreshing color, like the beautiful leaves of trees, and of course, there are many types and shades of green, each having its own charm.

In some verses of the Quran, such as verse 30 of Surah Al-Kahf, it is mentioned that the inhabitants of Paradise are adorned with bracelets of gold: "يُحَلَّوْنَ فِيها مِنْ أَساوِرَ مِنْ ذَهَبٍ." This does not contradict what is in the verse under discussion, as it is possible that for the sake of variety, they are sometimes adorned with this and sometimes with that.

Here the question arises: Are not gold and silver bracelets feminine ornaments? How are these ornaments mentioned for the men of Paradise? The answer is clear, as in many environments, gold and silver ornaments are for both men and women (although Islam has forbidden gold ornaments for men in this world). However, the types of bracelets for men and women are different. From verse 53 of Surah Az-Zukhruf, quoted from Pharaoh: "فَلَوْ لا أُلْقِيَ عَلَيْهِ أَسْوِرَةٌ مِنْ ذَهَبٍ" (Why then have not bracelets of gold been given to him [Moses]?), it appears that gold bracelets for men were considered a sign of greatness in the Egyptian environment.

Furthermore, as we have repeatedly pointed out, since the ordinary words of this world are never sufficient to express the blessings of Paradise, there is no way but to use these words as indications of those great and indescribable blessings.

Finally, at the end of the verse, as the last and most important blessing in this series of favors, He says: "And their Lord will give them a pure drink to drink" (وَ سَقاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ شَراباً طَهُوراً).

It is true that within these blessings, there was also mention of wholesome drinks from cups filled from the spring of Salsabīl, from which the inhabitants of Paradise are quenched, but there is a great difference between those and what is mentioned in this verse. For on one hand, the servers there were "وِلْدانٌ مُخَلَّدُونَ" (youths of everlasting youth), but here the server is "Allah." What an extraordinary expression! Especially with the emphasis on the word "Rabb" (Lord)—the God who has always nurtured this human being and is his Owner and Educator, who has constantly moved him forward through the stages of perfection until he reached the final stage. Now it is time for His Lordship to reach its zenith, and with the hand of His power, He quenches and delights the Righteous servants from the cup of pure drink.

On the other hand, "tahūr" means something that is both pure and purifying. Thus, this drink purifies the human body and soul from any kind of pollution and impurity, and grants him such spirituality, luminosity, and joy that its description fits no words. It is even narrated in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): "It purifies them from everything except Allah." [^1] It tears the veils of negligence, removes the barriers, and makes the human worthy of permanent presence in the proximity of God. The intoxication of this pure drink is superior to every blessing and higher than every favor.

If the polluted wine of the world destroys the intellect and distances man from God, the pure drink given by the hand of the "Eternal Server" makes him a stranger to "everything other than Allah" and drowns him in His Beauty and Majesty. In short, the grace hidden in this verse and in this blessing is superior and higher than all.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 411.

From a Hadith narrated from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), it is understood that the spring of pure drink is located at the gate of Paradise: "They are given a draught from it, and Allah thereby purifies their hearts from envy! ... and that is the word of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَ سَقاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ شَراباً طَهُوراً." "A draught of this pure drink is given to them, and through it, God purifies their hearts from envy (and all base qualities)." [^1] Interestingly, the term "tahūr" appears in the Holy Quran in only two instances: one regarding rain (Al-Furqan: 48) which purifies and gives life to everything, and the other in the verse under discussion regarding the special heavenly drink, which is also purifying and life-giving.

In the final verse under discussion, He states the concluding word in this regard, saying: "It will be said to them from Allah: Indeed, these great blessings and unique favors are a reward for your deeds, and your effort and struggle in the path of obeying the command of Truth is accepted and appreciated" (إِنَّ هذا كانَ لَكُمْ جَزاءً وَ كانَ سَعْيُكُمْ مَشْكُوراً). Lest anyone imagine that these great favors and rewards are given without account; these are all the recompense for effort and action, and the reward for struggles, self-refinement, and turning away from sin. [^2] The expression of this matter itself has a special pleasure and grace—that the Great God, or His angels, address the Righteous and, as a token of appreciation and thanks, say: "All these are the reward for your deeds, and your effort is appreciated." Rather, according to some commentators, this is a blessing above all blessings and a favor higher than all favors: that God thanks the human being.

The use of "kāna" (was), which is a past tense verb, informs of the past; it may be an indication that these blessings were prepared for you beforehand, for whenever someone places great importance on their guest, they prepare the means for their hosting long in advance.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 485, under Hadith 60. [^2]: This verse actually has an implied phrase such as "It is said to them" or "Allah says to them."

Ayahs

إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ تَنزِيلًا (٢٣)
Indeed We have sent down to you the Quran in a gradual descent. (23)
فَٱصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ وَلَا تُطِعْ مِنْهُمْ ءَاثِمًا أَوْ كَفُورًا (٢٤)
So submit patiently to the judgement of your Lord and do not obey any sinner or ingrate from among them, (24)
وَٱذْكُرِ ٱسْمَ رَبِّكَ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا (٢٥)
and celebrate the Name of your Lord morning and evening, (25)
وَمِنَ ٱلَّيْلِ فَٱسْجُدْ لَهُۥ وَسَبِّحْهُ لَيْلًا طَوِيلًا (٢٦)
and worship Him for a watch of the night and glorify Him the night long. (26)

Commentary

Five Important Commands for Success in Executing Allah's Decree

The verses of this Surah, from the beginning until now, spoke about the creation of man and then his Resurrection and the Day of Judgment. In the verses under discussion, the address is directed toward the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, giving him emphatic instructions for guiding humanity and for patience and steadfastness on this path. In fact, these verses show the way to attain all those unparalleled blessings, which is only possible through clinging to the Quran, following a leader like the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and drawing inspiration from his commands.

First, He says: "Indeed, it is We who have sent down the Quran to you progressively" (إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ تَنْزِيلًا).

Some commentators have considered "tanzila" (تَنْزِيلًا), which appears as an "absolute object" (maf'ul mutlaq) in the above verse, as an allusion to the "gradual revelation of the Quran," the educational effect of which is clear. Others consider it an allusion to the greatness of the status of this Heavenly Book and an emphasis on the revelation of the Quran from Allah's side, especially considering the other emphases present in the verse (emphasis through "inna," "nahnu," and the nominal sentence structure). In reality, it is a response to those who accused the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله of soothsaying, sorcery, and fabricating lies against Allah.

Then, He gives five important commands to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, the first of which is a call to patience and steadfastness. He says: "So be patient for the decision of your Lord" (فَاصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ). Since this is the case, be patient and enduring in the path of preaching and implementing the decrees of your Lord. Do not allow fear or dread of the difficulties, obstacles of the way, the multitude of enemies, and their stubbornness to enter your heart, and continue to move forward.

It is noteworthy that the command for steadfastness (considering the "fa" of consequence in "fasbir") is made a branch of the revelation of the Quran from Allah. That is, because Allah is your supporter, definitely remain steadfast in this path. The expression "Rab" (Lord) is another subtle allusion to this same meaning.

In the second command, He warns the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله against any kind of compromise with the deviants, saying: "And do not obey from among them any sinner or ungrateful disbeliever" (وَ لا تُطِعْ مِنْهُمْ آثِماً أَوْ كَفُوراً).

In reality, this second decree is an emphasis on the first, because the crowd of enemies tried through various means to draw the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله into compromise on the path of falsehood. As it has been narrated, 'Utbah ibn Rabi'ah and Walid ibn Mughirah used to say to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Desist from your call; we will place so much wealth at your disposal that you will be satisfied, and we will give you the most beautiful Arab girls in marriage," and other such proposals. As a true great leader, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله must exercise patience and steadfastness against these satanic temptations or the threats that are raised after these allurements prove ineffective; he must neither surrender to allurement nor to threat.

It is true that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله never surrendered, but this is an emphasis on the importance of this matter regarding his person صلى الله عليه وآله and an eternal model for other leaders on the path of Allah.

Although some commentators have interpreted "athim" (sinner) as 'Utbah ibn Rabi'ah and "kafur" (ungrateful disbeliever) as Walid ibn Mughirah or Abu Jahl—all three of whom were among the Arab polytheists—it is clear that "athim" and "kafur" have a broad and expansive meaning that includes all criminals and polytheists, even though these three individuals were clear examples of it.

It is also noteworthy that "athim" has a general meaning that also includes "kafur"; therefore, the mention of "kafur" is of the type of mentioning the "specific" after the "general" for the sake of emphasis.

However, since patience and steadfastness against the onslaught of these great difficulties is not an easy task and traversing this path requires two specific provisions, He adds in the next verse:

"And remember the name of your Lord morning and evening" (وَ اذْكُرِ اسْمَ رَبِّكَ بُكْرَةً وَ أَصِيلًا).

"And during the night prostrate to Him and exalt Him a long [part of the] night" (وَ مِنَ اللَّيْلِ فَاسْجُدْ لَهُ وَ سَبِّحْهُ لَيْلًا طَوِيلًا). So that under the shadow of that "remembrance" and this "prostration" and "glorification," you may provide the necessary strength, spiritual power, and sufficient support for struggling against the difficulties of this path.

"Bukrah" (on the weight of 'nuktah') means the beginning of the day, and "asil" is its opposite, meaning the evening and the end of the day.

Some have said that applying this word to the end of the day—considering it is derived from the root "asl" (origin/foundation)—is because the end of the day forms the foundation and basis of the night.

From some expressions, it is understood that "asil" is sometimes applied to the interval between noon and sunset (Raghib's Mufradat).

From others, it appears that "asil" is also said for the early parts of the night, because they have interpreted it as "ashi," and "ashi" is the beginning of the night, just as the Maghrib and Isha prayers are called "asha'ayn." It is even understood from some words that "ashi" includes from the decline of the sun (noon) until the next morning [1]. However, considering that "asil" in the noble verse is placed opposite "bukrah" (morning), and after it, there is talk of nightly worship, it becomes clear that the meaning is that same end part of the day.

[1]: "Mufradat al-Raghib".

In any case, these two verses are actually expressive of the necessity of constant, day-and-night attention to the Holy Essence of the Lord.

Some have interpreted it specifically as the five daily prayers, or plus the Night Prayer (Salat al-Layl), or specifically the Dawn, Afternoon, Sunset, and Night prayers. However, it appears that these prayers are instances of this continuous divine remembrance, and the glorification and prostration before His Holy Presence.

The expression "laylan tawila" (a long night) refers to the fact that you should glorify Allah for a large portion of the night. In a Hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام regarding its interpretation, it is mentioned that in response to the question "What is meant by this glorification?", he said: "It means the Night Prayer" [1].

However, it is not unlikely that this interpretation is also of the type of stating a clear instance, because the Night Prayer has an extraordinary effect on strengthening the spirit of faith, refining the soul, and keeping the human will alive on the path of obedience to Allah.

At this point, one must pay attention to the fact that although the five commands in the above verses are mentioned as a program for the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, they are in reality a model for all those who step into the path of spiritual and human leadership of human society. They must know that after complete certainty and faith in the goal and mission they have, it is necessary to adopt patience and steadfastness and not be terrified by the mass of difficulties on the way. This is because guiding a population—especially when ignorant individuals and stubborn enemies stand before a person—has always been and is accompanied by great difficulties.

[1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 413.

And if the patience and steadfastness of leaders were not present, no mission would reach fruition.

In the next stage, they must resist with full power against the temptations of the devils who are instances of the "sinner" (athim) and "ungrateful" (kafur), and who try to deviate the leaders and guides with various tricks and deceptions so that their mission remains sterile. They must neither be deceived by allurements nor allow any dread of threats into themselves.

And in all stages, to gain spiritual power, willpower, firm resolve, and iron determination, they should remember Allah every morning and evening and rub their foreheads at His threshold, especially seeking help from nightly worships and intimate supplication with Him; for if these matters are observed, victory is certain.

And if in some stages a calamity or defeat occurs, they can be compensated for in the light of these principles. The life program of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and his call and mission is an effective model for the travelers of this path.

Ayahs

إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ يُحِبُّونَ ٱلْعَاجِلَةَ وَيَذَرُونَ وَرَآءَهُمْ يَوْمًا ثَقِيلًا (٢٧)
Indeed they love this transitory life, and disregard a heavy day that is ahead of them. (27)
نَّحْنُ خَلَقْنَـٰهُمْ وَشَدَدْنَآ أَسْرَهُمْ ۖ وَإِذَا شِئْنَا بَدَّلْنَآ أَمْثَـٰلَهُمْ تَبْدِيلًا (٢٨)
We created them and strengthened their joints, and We will replace them with others like them whenever We like. (28)
إِنَّ هَـٰذِهِۦ تَذْكِرَةٌ ۖ فَمَن شَآءَ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ سَبِيلًا (٢٩)
This is indeed a reminder. So let anyone who wishes take the way toward his Lord. (29)
وَمَا تَشَآءُونَ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (٣٠)
But you will not wish unless it is willed by Allah. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (30)
يُدْخِلُ مَن يَشَآءُ فِى رَحْمَتِهِۦ ۚ وَٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ أَعَدَّ لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًۢا (٣١)
He admits whomever He wishes into His mercy, and He has prepared a painful punishment for the wrongdoers. (31)

Commentary

This is a Warning, and the Choice of the Path is Yours!

In the preceding verses, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was warned never to be influenced by the "sinners" (آثم) and the "disbelievers" (كفور). As history testifies, these were individuals who, in their crude imagination, sought to influence the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) will and decision-making by resorting to enticements of wealth, status, and beautiful women.

The verses under discussion further identify them, stating: "Indeed, these [disbelievers] love the fleeting life of this world and leave behind them [neglecting] a heavy Day" (إِنَّ هؤُلاءِ يُحِبُّونَ الْعاجِلَةَ وَ يَذَرُونَ وَراءَهُمْ يَوْماً ثَقِيلًا).

The horizon of their thoughts does not extend beyond eating, sleeping, and lust; the ultimate point of their vision is these unconditional material pleasures. Surprisingly, they wish to measure the great soul of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) by this same standard.

However, these oblivious and blind-hearted people do not realize what a heavy Day lies ahead of them—heavy in terms of punishments, heavy in terms of accounting, and heavy in terms of its duration and the weight of disgrace and ignominy.

The expression "behind them" (وراءهم), even though the Day is technically ahead of them, is used because they have consigned that Day to oblivion, as if they have cast it behind their backs. Nevertheless, according to some commentators, the word wara’ is sometimes used to mean "behind" and sometimes "ahead" [1].

In the next verse, He warns them not to be arrogant regarding their own strength and power, for all of these have been granted by Allah, and He can take them back swiftly whenever He wills. He says: "We created them and strengthened their forms, and when We will, We can replace them with others like them" (نَحْنُ خَلَقْناهُمْ وَ شَدَدْنا أَسْرَهُمْ وَ إِذا شِئْنا بَدَّلْنا أَمْثالَهُمْ تَبْدِيلًا) [2].

Asr (rhyming with asr) originally means binding something with a chain. Captives are called asir because they are bound. However, asr here refers to the firming of the connections within the human being's existence, which grants him the power of movement and the ability to perform significant activities.

Truly, the Quran here points to a sensitive reality: the various connections between the components of human existence, ranging from the small and large nerves that link muscles together like iron ropes, to the ligaments and various muscles.

[1]: In the commentary Ruh al-Bayan, it is mentioned that if wara’ is annexed to the "subject" (doer), it means "behind," and if it is annexed to the "object," it means "ahead" (Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 8, p. 439).

[2]: This verse has an omitted part (ellipses) and is estimated as: baddalnahum amthalahum (Note that "replacement" usually takes two objects; here the pronoun hum is the first object and amthalahum is the second).

These small and large pieces of bone and flesh in the human body are so firmly bound together that they form a completely integrated unit ready for any activity. Overall, however, this phrase is a metaphor for power and strength.

This verse also clarifies the independence and self-sufficiency of Allah's Holy Essence from them and from their obedience or faith, so they may know that if there is insistence on their faith, it is in fact a grace and mercy from the Lord.

A similar meaning appears in Surah Al-An'am, verse 133, where He says: وَ رَبُّكَ الْغَنِيُّ ذُو الرَّحْمَةِ إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ وَ يَسْتَخْلِفْ مِنْ بَعْدِكُمْ ما يَشاءُ: "And your Lord is the Self-Sufficient, full of mercy. If He wills, He can do away with you and concede to whom He wills to succeed you."

Then, referring to all the discussions presented in this Surah, which together offer a comprehensive program for salvation, He says: "Indeed, this is a reminder; so whoever wills, let him take a path to his Lord" (إِنَّ هذِهِ تَذْكِرَةٌ فَمَنْ شاءَ اتَّخَذَ إِلى‌ رَبِّهِ سَبِيلًا) [1]. Our duty is to show the way, not to force the choice. It is you who must distinguish truth from falsehood using your intellect and understanding, and decide by your own will and choice. This is, in fact, an emphasis on what was stated at the beginning of the Surah:

[1]: Some assume an omitted word in the verse, suggesting it means: "whoever wills, let him take a path to the pleasure of his Lord" or "to the obedience of his Lord." But in fairness, there is no need for such an estimation, as all paths of perfection lead toward Allah.

إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شاكِراً وَ إِمَّا كَفُوراً: "Indeed, We guided him to the way, be he grateful or ungrateful."

Since narrow-minded individuals might perceive the above expression as a form of absolute delegation (tafwid) to the servants, He adds in the next verse to negate this illusion: "And you do not will except that Allah wills" (وَ ما تَشاؤُنَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللَّهُ‌) [1], "Indeed, Allah is ever All-Knowing and All-Wise" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ عَلِيماً حَكِيماً).

This is, in reality, an affirmation of the well-known principle of "the matter between the two matters" (al-amr bayn al-amrayn). On one hand, He says: "Allah has shown the way and the choice is yours," and on the other hand, He adds: "Your choice is dependent on the Divine Will." This means you do not have absolute independence; rather, your power, ability, and freedom of will are all by the will of Allah and from Him, and whenever He wills, He can withdraw this power and freedom.

Thus, it is neither "delegation" (complete handover) nor "compulsion" (deprivation of choice), but a subtle and delicate reality between the two. Or, in other words: it is a type of freedom dependent on the Divine Will, which He can take back at any moment He chooses, so that servants can both carry the burden of duty and responsibility—which is the secret of their perfection—and not imagine themselves independent of Allah.

In short, this expression is so that servants do not consider themselves independent of His guidance,

[1]: Regarding the grammatical position of "أَنْ يَشاءَ اللَّهُ‌", some commentators say it is in the accusative of time (zarf), meaning "you do not will except at the time of Allah's will." However, it is also possible that "something" (shay'an) is implied, meaning "you do not will anything except that which Allah wills."

support, success, and confirmation of His Holy Essence. While making decisions in their affairs, they should entrust themselves to Him and place themselves under His protection.

From this, it becomes clear that some fatalistic commentators, such as Fakhr al-Razi, have clung to this verse because of their preconceived notions on this issue. He says: "Know that this verse is among those verses in which the waves of fatalism (jabr) and predestination clash!" [1]. Yes, if we separate this verse from the preceding ones, there is room for such an illusion. But considering that one verse expresses the effect of choice and another expresses the effect of the Lord's will, the principle of "the matter between the two matters" is firmly established.

It is strange that proponents of absolute delegation cling to the verse that speaks of absolute choice, while proponents of fatalism cling to the verse that, in isolation, smells of compulsion. Each seeks to justify their preconceived notions with it, whereas a correct understanding of the Divine Word (or any other speech) requires putting them all together and judging without prejudice or prior bias.

The end of the verse, which says: "Indeed, Allah is ever All-Knowing and All-Wise," may also point to this same meaning. For Allah's knowledge and wisdom require that He leaves the servants free to traverse the path of perfection; otherwise, forced and imposed perfection is not perfection at all. Furthermore, His knowledge and wisdom do not allow Him to compel some to do good and others to do evil, and then reward the first group and punish the second.

[1]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 30, p. 262.

Finally, in the last verse of this Surah, He points to the fate of the righteous and the wicked in a short and meaningful sentence, saying: "He admits whom He wills into His mercy; but for the wrongdoers, He has prepared a painful punishment" (يُدْخِلُ مَنْ يَشاءُ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ وَ الظَّالِمِينَ أَعَدَّ لَهُمْ عَذاباً أَلِيماً).

It is interesting that at the beginning of the verse He says: "He admits whom He wills into His mercy," but at the end of the verse, He focuses the punishment on the "wrongdoers" (zalimin). This shows that His will for punishment follows the human's will for wrongdoing and sin. By the principle of contrast, it becomes clear that His will for mercy also follows the human's will for faith, righteous deeds, and the practice of justice; nothing else can be expected from the All-Wise.

It is surprising that despite this clear context, individuals like Fakhr al-Razi have taken the beginning of the verse as evidence for fatalism without paying attention to the end of the verse, which speaks about the freedom of will and the actions of the wrongdoers! [1]

O Allah! Admit us into Your mercy and save us from the painful punishment that awaits the wrongdoers. O Lord! You have shown the way, and we have decided to traverse this path; help us! O God! If we are not among the "righteous" (abrar), we are lovers of them; join us with them.

End of Surah al-Insan (Dahr) 1 Rabi' al-Thani 1407

[1]: For more details regarding the verses of "Will" (Mashiyyah), please refer to Vol. 19, pp. 461-468 (under verse 37 of Surah Az-Zumar).
